schools.summary: Keil sets the verse at the head of Rehoboam's consolidation; Ellicott notes the chronicler omits the call of Jeroboam (1 Kings 12:20) and abridges 1 Kings 12:21.
Gill treats vv.1-4 as matter already told, referring the reader to the parallel in Kings.
Henry weighs the muster morally: Rehoboam acts boldly, but a war against God's decree cannot prosper.
Clarke lays out the chapter's shape and refers the details to the parallel at 1 Kings 12.
Keil takes the verse as the opening of the oracle (vv.2-4) that turned Rehoboam from war to consolidation; Ellicott notes the restored divine name and the fuller form Shemayahu (cf. 2 Chron 10:15).

themes: the word of the LORD to Shemaiah

schools.summary: Ellicott hears in 'king of Judah' the seal of divine assent, and weighs 'all Israel in Judah and Benjamin' as the surviving true Israel.
Wesley reads 'son of Solomon' as intimating the division was determined for Solomon's sin and could not be reversed.
Ellicott renders 'this thing is done of me' as 'from me arose this matter' (cf. 2 Chron 10:15); Keil marks the great levy disbanded at the prophet's word.
Henry makes the obedience the moral heart: like a conscientious man Rehoboam let the war fall because God forbade it.

themes: the danger of war against God's will

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'embattled cities' and insists 'in Judah' means the kingdom, since some forts lay in Benjamin (v.10).
Gill fixes Jerusalem as the capital from which the fortifying radiated.
Clarke supplies the motive — frontiers braced against Israel, as Jeroboam braced his; Wesley notes 'built' means repaired and fortified, not founded.
Keil and Ellicott identify Bethlehem (birthplace of David and Christ), Etam (Ain Attan, distinct from the Simeonite Etam), and Tekoa; 'built' means fortified.
Gill marks Bethlehem as the place of our Lord's birth, six miles from Jerusalem.
Keil and Ellicott locate Beth-zur, Shoco, and Adullam, agreeing on the first two and leaving Adullam's exact site an open question.
Gill notes all three lay in the tribe of Judah.
Keil and Ellicott keep the famous Philistine Gath (a vassal city under Solomon, cf. 1 Kings 2:39; 1 Chron 18:1) and locate Mareshah and Ziph in the Judean hills.
Gill judges this Gath a Judean town, not the Philistine capital, perhaps Moresheth-gath.

debates.question: Is the Gath here the Philistine capital or a lesser Judean town?

sides.position: the famous Philistine royal city, now a subject city drawn into the defences
a lesser town of Judah of the same name, perhaps Moresheth-gath

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott identify Adoraim (Dura), Lachish (Um Lakis), and Azekah (near Socoh), tracing their after-history through later wars.
Gill fixes Adoraim by the Adora of Josephus.
Keil and Ellicott identify Zorah, Aijalon (once Danite, later Benjamite), and Hebron; Ellicott, citing Bertheau, reads the list as forts against the south and west — Egypt — not the north.
Gill glosses Zorah as the place elsewhere called Zoreah.

themes: the fear that shaped the list

schools.summary: Keil unfolds the measures — commandants, provisions, arms — and reads them as rounding off the account of Rehoboam's consolidation (cf. 2 Chron 10:19); Ellicott glosses the terms.
Gill takes the verse plainly of the fortifying of the cities.
Clarke sees a double prudence in the stores: to endure a siege and to supply desolate districts.
Keil reads the arms as the seal of the fortification account; Ellicott renders 'there fell to him Judah and Benjamin' as a definition of the territory (cf. 2 Chron 10:17).
Gill takes shields and spears for all the weapons of war, to defend and to annoy.
Keil explains the Levites 'went over' to Rehoboam, driven by Jeroboam's calf-worship at Bethel and Dan; Ellicott glosses 'resorted' and 'coasts.'
Henry reads the migration as a lesson on the fellowship of the faithful — refugees who loved their work above their maintenance, welcomed by a wise king.
Gill notes the priests and Levites came from their cities throughout the ten tribes.

themes: the true strengthening of the kingdom

schools.summary: Ellicott glosses the pasture-grounds and cities left behind and explains Jeroboam's casting-off (1 Kings 12:26-31); Ellicott and Keil read 'his sons' as his successors.
Clarke reads the expulsion as policy to prevent revolt; Wesley weighs the Levites' costly refusal to serve the calves.
Gill reckons the forty-eight Levitical cities the priests gave up.
Keil and Ellicott expose the 'devils' as goat-demons (se'irim, cf. Lev 17:7; Isa 13:21); Ellicott holds the calves represented the God of Israel, a syncretism.
Clarke traces the goats and calves to Egyptian idolatry (Apis); Wesley distinguishes high places for the Baals and for the calves by which Jeroboam pretended to serve the true God.
Gill notes the priests were men of no Levitical or Aaronic line.

debates.question: Was the calf-worship a corrupt cult of the true God or plain Egyptian idolatry?

sides.position: a syncretism — the calves represented the God of Israel, worshipped under an image
sheer heathenism after the Egyptian pattern (the ox-god Apis, goat-demons)

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott show the devout came not to visit but to settle (nathan lebab, cf. 1 Chron 22:19), like the migrations under Asa and Hezekiah.
Clarke reckons the influx: all the truly pious, the whole tribe of Levi, and part of Simeon.
Henry reads their choice as the pattern of right priorities — 'that is best for us which is best for our souls.'
Gill notes the common people came after the priests' example, out of all ten tribes.
Keil grounds the three years' strength in the religious foundation and the emigration of the godly; Ellicott reads 'the way of David and Solomon' as the chronicler ignoring Solomon's late apostasy.
Henry: the faithful procured a blessing that made Rehoboam strong only while they walked in the good way — 'we retain our strength while we cleave to God.'
Clarke fixes the span — three faithful years, fourteen unfaithful after; Wesley takes the praise of Solomon as evidence of his true repentance before death.
Gill notes the immigrants made the kingdom more numerous and defensible.

debates.question: What does the praise of 'the way of David and Solomon' imply about Solomon?

sides.position: a considerable evidence of Solomon's true repentance before his death
the chronicler's selective art, ignoring Solomon's late foreign religions

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read 'the daughter' (not son) of Jerimoth, a likely son of a concubine, and take Abihail as Mahalath's mother, not a second wife.
Gill notes this Jerimoth, a son of David, is nowhere else named.
Clarke draws the dynastic point: marrying within David's family strengthened Rehoboam's right to the throne.

debates.question: Is Abihail Mahalath's mother or a second wife of Rehoboam?

sides.position: Mahalath's mother; only one wife is named (singular verbs in vv.19-20)

holders: the Septuagint and Vulgate (as reported by Ellicott)

themes: marrying within the house of David

schools.summary: Ellicott makes Mahalath the subject of 'she bare' and notes the three sons occur nowhere else; Keil sets Jeush, Shamariah, and Zaham in place before turning to Maachah.
Gill inclines to make Abihail the mother, the other wife perhaps dying childless.
Keil and Ellicott, with Josephus, make Maachah the granddaughter of Absalom through Tamar and Uriel, reconciling 'daughter of Absalom' with 'daughter of Uriel' (2 Chron 13:2).
Gill flags the improbability of a literal daughter of Absalom, who had but one daughter, Tamar.
Clarke reports the harmonization and the Targum's gloss on the change of name.

debates.question: Was Maachah Absalom's daughter or granddaughter?

sides.position: his granddaughter, daughter of Tamar and Uriel of Gibeah
not his literal daughter, since Absalom had but one daughter, Tamar

outliers.note: Passes on the Targum's legend that Maachah, an upright woman, had her name changed to Michaiah and her father renamed Uriel, so that Absalom's name might not be remembered.

themes: the blood of David and Absalom

schools.summary: Keil numbers the wives and marks the echo of Solomon; Ellicott notes the later verb for 'took' and finds the round 'threescore concubines' suspicious beside Josephus' thirty.
Clarke reckons eighty-eight children and pities their likely neglect — Rehoboam 'bad enough, but not so abandoned as his father.'
Gill identifies the favourite as the Michaiah of the later genealogy.

debates.question: How many concubines did Rehoboam have?

sides.position: threescore (sixty), as the Hebrew text reads

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott render 'appointed Abijah for head, for prince (nagid) among his brethren,' with the purpose of making him king.
Clarke holds the preference of a younger son broke the law of Deut 21:16, excused by David's example; Wesley reads it simply as declaring Abijah successor.
Gill takes Abijah for the chief, son of the best-loved wife.

debates.question: Did designating Abijah violate the law of the firstborn (Deut 21:15-17)?

sides.position: yes, a breach excused only by David's precedent in preferring Solomon
holders: the Speaker's Commentary (as reported by Ellicott)

sides.position: no breach — the firstborn's right was only a double portion, not the throne

themes: choosing a younger son to reign

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read the dispersal as political sagacity — sons settled as governors with maintenance and wives, kept content and apart to forestall intrigue (cf. 2 Chron 21:2-3).
Clarke sees policy in scattering the sons beyond leagues with Jeroboam; Wesley, in preventing a union against Abijah after the king's death.
Gill canvasses the senses of 'dealt wisely' and settles it of Rehoboam himself.

debates.question: What is the sense of 'he dealt wisely'?

sides.position: of Rehoboam, who prudently dispersed and provided for his sons
of Abijah, whom the king made to understand, or who himself understood
